claim: A 2012 review documents the reduction in Bangladesh cyclone mortality from 300,000-500,000 in 1970 to 3,406 in 2007 Cyclone Sidr through the establishment of the Cyclone Preparedness Programme, early warning dissemination, and cyclone shelter construction.

source.title: Reduced death rates from cyclones in Bangladesh: what more needs to be done?

method: Reviewed WHO Bulletin article mortality analysis, CPP description, and shelter infrastructure assessment.
limitations: Historical mortality data before 1970 is estimated, not precise.
Review does not quantify modern false-alarm rates.

claim: Bangladesh's Cyclone Preparedness Programme operates 76,000 volunteers across coastal districts, maintains over 14,000 cyclone shelters, and uses a flag-based plus mobile-phone early warning dissemination system that reaches most but not all coastal communities.
method: Reviewed CPP website for program structure, volunteer numbers, shelter inventory, and warning dissemination methods.
limitations: Official program data may overstate effective coverage for hardest-to-reach communities.
Shelter capacity figures may not reflect actual usable space during a cyclone.

claim: A 2020 analysis of Bay of Bengal cyclone track forecast errors finds mean errors of 50-80 km at 24 hours and 80-120 km at 48 hours, with intensification forecasts showing larger errors than track forecasts.

source.title: Evaluation of operational tropical cyclone track and intensity forecasts in the Bay of Bengal

method: Reviewed abstract, forecast methodology, track error statistics by lead time, and intensity error analysis.
limitations: Forecast errors reflect operational model performance during the study period, which may have improved since 2020.
Track errors are basin averages and may differ for specific districts.
